Actually, it seems to be clearer to keep the separation between the links and the chain, and to configure how the chain is using the links. 
This allows users to declare the links once and reuse them in different chains, activated or not. For example, in the Poppy torso, the ankle is part of every chains, but only active in the `bust` chain. 
Since there are many different use cases, sticking to the original API is the best option to cover everything. 
So, since it's not the point of this PR, i suggest to revert back to the original link format, which already permits to set a link as non active (the active links mask).
Maybe we will add in another PR a nicer interface to the "non-active" links, but it requires some more thought for now.

Bye,

I'm afraid I'm going to need a bit more information. In other systems, 3.1 is running just fine on Python 2.7.3 and 2.7.5. Here's where I would investigate:

* Could it be that the first system had a config file that specified a particular backend? Make sure that backend includes the full path to the desired backend. Try without the config file.
* Confirm that the module actually exists. Find where keyring is installed and look for 'keyring/backends/file.py'.

I was going to suggest other things to try like importing 'keyring.backends.file' manually, but I realize now there's no way to get at the backends without causing everything to be imported (as a side-effect of `import keyring`).

It's conceivable there's a race condition when importing a certain backend before another. However, I wouldn't expect that to happen using the default configuration (as the import behavior is very similar for all platforms) - so I'd still suspect the behavior is triggered by something in configuration.

Please let me know what you find.

@mark-adams My apologies for the delayed answer.

The issue at had is how to share the version number between the package itself and the metadata. Currently there are two possibilities:
1. put the version in the setup.py (metadata) - As implemented by this pull request.
2. put the version in the package.

The reasons why options 1 is better are:
1. A single place for the version - eliminate duplication and reduces chances for errors (version = latest tag).
2. Provides a distinct PEP440 compliant version for every unofficial build.
4. Eliminate redundant code from setup.py (DRY - don't repeat yourself).
5. Shorten release procedure (tag, sdist, upload).

The perceived negatives are the the setup_requires dependencies. Which is irrelevant when providing wheels.
 
@jpadilla advocates 2. Which does not have the above additional advantages but is a solution to the problem. 
In addition, The recommended implementation differs from the one suggested by @jpadilla. The recommended way is to place a file inside the package (_version.py) containing the version into the package and place the following into setup.py:
```python
_locals = {}
with open('jwt/_version.py') as fp:
    exec(fp.read(), None, _locals)
__version__ = _locals['__version__']
```
Simple and compatible with any [pep 440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/). 

A good discussion can be found [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2058802/how-can-i-get-the-version-defined-in-setup-py-setuptools-in-my-package).

class FullUnparam(Generic[T, U]):
    pass

class HalfParam(FullUnparam[T, int]):
    pass

class EntirelyParam(FullUnparam[str, int]):
    pass

# This works with FullUnparam.__subclasses__() today
assert get_subclasses(FullUnparam) == [HalfParam, EntirelyParam]

# variant 1:  only Generic subclasses
assert get_subclasses(FullUnparam,only_generics=True) == [HalfParam]

# variant 2: only Generic subclasses with same parameters
assert get_subclasses(FullUnparam, only_generics=True, parametrized=False) == []

Wow. Looks like neither the ipaddr nor the state is an issue, but it is the IPDB restart that screws all the things.

The IPDB doesn't restart by itself, it is a netlink socket error that triggers the restart. The socket error may come if the netlink client doesn't manage to handle all the incoming messages.

The IPDB normally runs the netlink messages fetching code as a separate thread. The code doesn't parse the messages, only buffers them to the parser (see also `IPDB._nl_async`). If there is a message burst, you'll get in the logs (if the logging is set up): https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/master/pyroute2/netlink/nlsocket.py#L43

If you get the ENOBUF error (https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/master/pyroute2/netlink/nlsocket.py#L23) it may mean that the IPDB runs on the top of `NetNS` (that doesn't respect `_nl_async` 'cause of the architectural issues), not `IPRoute` — is it the case? Or maybe you manually set `_nl_async` to `False` somehow?

If it is the case, maybe it is better to spawn normal IPDB _inside_ netns, like
1. spawn a child
2. setns()
3. instantiate IPDB() w/o any additional parameters

> Indeed, abstracting something to the point of being easy to use at the expense of some form of degradation (such as performance or resource control) is seen in quite a few popular libraries

If you take a look at many popular libraries, for instance, SDL; a lot will include signal handling, and in the case of SDL and many others, they also include a way to turn it off; looking at the library code, you would only have to possibly pass an additional argument (e.g. `handle_signal`) and check against it before handling.

Additionally, this issue brings up something else: there _is_ a notable inconsistency between running the same exact code on different platforms: You can use `run()` in its own thread just fine on Windows since there are no signals supported for that platform, but the moment you'll switch to e.g. Linux, it'll blow up in your face.

This is an excellent opportunity to both allow your users to have more control on the function (you can always have it handle by default when nothing is passed), and make the system more consistent across platforms (that is, if you actually consider there are better options and feel like implementing them).

And all things considered, it would in no way degrade usability.

After investigation your software behave as ansible does.
It turns out ec2.py can create several cache profiles (one per account), but because of the way it is invoked with ansible, it is using the same cache (a third one if you have 2 accounts/cache profiles) which is the no-profile one. If your cache policy is 5 minutes, you will not notice this if you work with an account one day and another the following day. But if you switch back and forth, several times an hour you will. (as I did!). Maybe a solution is to use 2 wrapper scripts around ec2.py (which would call respectively ec2.py with the hardcoded AWS profiles). I send an email to the ansible google group to see if there is a better solution. In any case, your software behave like ansible does. Thanks,

> I also wanted to ask, what is the strategy with the deprecated options? At some point options become deprecated, but are they ever getting removed from the codebase, e.g. after two consequent releases? 

Currently there is no strategy, the user will only know if they look in the logs.  But notifying the user just causes them pain.  The proposed solution is.  We notify the user that their config needs updating due to config changes (via i3-nagbar) they can then click either `I don't care` or `fix my config`.  But for this we need to be able to fix the config.  This means we need to do a much better job of parsing the config, so we know where the setting is so that we can fix it.  We could just dump a fresh config but then they would lose any comments etc that they had which is unacceptable.

So that is what we are aiming for but till we get that we'll just carry the deprecations.  I've done some of the work but it needs another burst and is quite a hard problem to do seamlessly.  Once we have a parser that remembers where in the file it got things from then the updating the config should be fairly simple.  Once we have that then we can look at when we remove the deprications.

Another option might actually be to start to move the deprecations out of the modules themselves so that the modules become clean again even if the deprecations stay somewhere else in the code base.  But yeah we should be looking to remove them or at least from the modules.

With pip >= 6.0 the OSX tags probably become a bit less of a mess:

Yeah, there's a lot going on in that function, which could probably be broken out a bit. 

So with the old method (`PostMedia()`), the API just assumed that you were posting a photo (cause, at the time, that's all you could post to twitter) and so `read()`ing in a stream of data made sense. Now the API can handle GIFs and videos and each of those have different file-size limitations and attachment limitations, so we try to figure out what you want to upload before uploading the data. 

The problem with using a StringIO buffer to post a photo or video is that there's no way (that I know of) of determining if the buffer contains a photo or video or GIF. (Off the top of my head, there may be a way you could probably do it by reading the first bytes of the buffer and then guessing the file type from that, but I don't know without further research.) So this doesn't have anything to do with Twitter's deprecation of that endpoint, just with our manner of figuring out what a user wants to upload. 

As for a solution, you can write the buffer out to a `NamedTemporaryFile` with the extension you want and then pass that to `PostUpdate` since it'll have a read and mode attribute. This is effectively what we do when we get a URL (check out the `http_to_file()` method in `twitter_utils.py`).

>  I am not sure I understand why ContextVars is a good thing.

Suppose you have a numerical library and you need to set options for handling NaNs or rounding or precision or whatever. So you use a context for that. However, if your code is async and you `await` inside that context, you'd expect that context to be still there after you return – otherwise Bad Things happen.

So that's why you need contexts in general. And as soon as you have support for contexts, well, then you get the ability to use that to store yet more context (in this case: which loop you're in) for free.

It is used as an argument to the gpg `--passphrase` flag and quoted in the construction of the command so that, even if a user/attacker were to enter a character that would allow an executable to run (e.g. entering a pipe symbol followed by a nefarious command) the shell will prevent it.  It is treated as a string argument in the shell.  Can you think of an example command that would thwart this mechanism and lead to problems?

If our `__init__.py` is:
```
from .core import Vulture
assert Vulture

__version__ = '0.14'
```

Then this would lead to `__init.py__` importing from `core` and `core` importing `__version__` from `__init__.py`, Thus circular imports!

This can be prevented though, if our `__init__.py` file looks like:
```
__version__ = '0.14'

from .core import Vulture
assert Vulture
```

In "modern" (es6) web development you wouldn't load a library by adding another script tag, but just npm install it and import it wherever needed. Then you have webpack/JSPM to take care of bundling all the stuff together.

So installing the client side part with pip doesn't make sense any more. While you could probably import js files by path it's unnecessary complex to do so for ``.js`` files that are somewhere in ``python/site-packages`` directory.

So to go with the flow the client part of django-angular2 has to be available via npm, I would also suggest making a separate repository for it.

Hi @Barathwaja, it looks like the data you downloaded isn't in a format that ChatterBot can read. You can still use this data but you will need to find a way to convert it to match the format that ChatterBot's training classes expect.

Here is a short example of the format that ChatterBot expects it's corpus data to be in:

```json

{
    "conversations": [
        [
            "Good morning, how are you?",
            "I am doing well, how about you?",
            "I'm also good.",
            "That's good to hear.",
            "Yes it is."
        ],
        [
            "How are you doing?",
            "I am doing well, how about you?",
            "I am also good.",
            "That's good."
        ],
        [
            "Have you heard the news?",
            "What good news?"
        ],
        [
            "What is your favorite book?",
            "I can't read.",
            "So what's your favorite color?",
            "Blue"
        ]
    ]
}
```

There is more information on the current set of available corpus trainers for ChatterBot in the documentation http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/training.html

Amazon does have some requirements for audio files. The documentation for the [AudioPlayer interface](https://developer.amazon.com/public/solutions/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/docs/custom-audioplayer-interface-reference#play) mentions the following:

> The audio file must be hosted at an Internet-accessible HTTPS endpoint. HTTPS is required, and the domain hosting the files must present a valid, trusted SSL certificate. Self-signed certificates cannot be used. Many content hosting services provide this. For example, you could host your files at a service such as Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) (an Amazon Web Services offering).
>
>The supported formats for the audio file include AAC/MP4, MP3, HLS, PLS and M3U. Bitrates: 16kbps to 384 kbps.

I believe there may be another caveat that the hosting endpoint must support CORS for requests from `http://ask-ifr-download.s3.amazonaws.com` per the [skill card documentation](https://developer.amazon.com/public/solutions/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/docs/providing-home-cards-for-the-amazon-alexa-app#image_hosting)

It's not always clear in the documentation, but I've found HTTPS and CORS tend to be a requirement for any media Amazon is going to present to the user either as part of the card or audio. Using S3 doesn't always automatically suffice as you may need to still enable CORS per the skill card doc linked above.

Hi, I cannot recreate you environment so some of this is a guess. 

From the message it seems clear that check_swarm was able to speak with your docker daemon, otherwise it would have giving a connection error. The message seems to point to the fact the TLS certificate presented by the remote docker daemon is not trusted. Unless you went through special effort to obtain a certificate it is likely 'self signed'. 

You likely need to do one of the following to resolve this.

- Install the self signed certificate in your systems root store.
- Obtain a 'real certificate' (likely purchased) and install in the docker daemon.
- Use the docker socket file since I see you are connecting on localhost (note: you cannot purchase certificates for localhost).
- Turn on a unencrypted listener in docker daemon and switch to using a unsecured connection, `--connection`

Googling for topics on 'self-signed certificates' and 'how SSL certificates work' may help you understand the issue this message is about.

It seems a little inconsistent that `released` and `closed` are returned but that there's no `bound` (or `opened` for that matter). The inconsistency doesn't make sense at the protocol level, and only makes sense together with certain decisions about client implementation. 

I think having the protocol be internally consistent is a good thing (it's easier to understand, and makes for fewer special cases in code).

One way to address this is to do something like zsync (http://zsync.moria.org.uk/).  The sender generates it dynamically and sends it, then the receiver can figure out what parts it needs.  This way you don't have to worry about reusing connections or codes, it automatically works for partial files.

Yes. I'm a little confused by your question, since you already have direct access to the nodes by iterating over them as you did.

Generally you are interested in a specific type of node, not everything in the page. For this you can use `for node in parsed_code.filter_tags():` (or `filter_templates()`, or `filter_wikilinks()`, or any method described [here](http://mwparserfromhell.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/mwparserfromhell.html#mwparserfromhell.wikicode.Wikicode.filter)).

The nodes themselves have attributes as described in the documentation you linked. So if you loop over tags, for example, you can do:

```python
for tag in parsed_code.filter_tags():
    print(tag.tag)  # Prints the name of the tag
    print(tag.contents)  # Prints the content inside of the tag
```

You can also just do `print(tag)` to print the whole thing out as a string.

In Django, the act of accessing the session marks the whole view as being
"per user", which adds a Vary: Cookie header onto the response. This has
the effect of effectively preventing the page being cached, so that
different users see different versions of the page.

Thus, reversion will only mark a revision with the current user if some
other part of your app has already accessed the session. Otherwise, the
effect of adding RevisionMiddleware would be to make your entire site
uncacheable, which is rarely what's desired.

If session.accessed is False, then your view isn't actually accessing
request.user anywhere. A simple call of `request.user.username` somewhere
should trigger it.

Alternatively, you could just create a modified version of
RevisionMiddleware that doesn't perform this check. However, it will break
caching across your entire site!

TBH, the RevisionMiddleware is not a recommend approach any more. You
really want to wrap all revisions in a transaction, which is impossible
using the RevisionMIddleware ever since Django dropped support for
TransactionMiddleware.

This is actually a limitation of pytz based on a implementation decision of dateutil ([see PEP-431](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0431/) It just does not work for tzinfo timezones with daylight time saving. Example, two datetimes, same zone only one in PST, the other in PDT, if converted to UTC they should have a 1 hour difference. The generated datetimes even land 7 minutes sooner than they should.

```python
In [1]: import datetime

In [2]: from pytz import timezone

In [3]: utczone = timezone('UTC')

In [4]: pacific = timezone('US/Pacific')

In [5]: datetime_in_pst = datetime.datetime(2015,3,4,9,0,0, tzinfo=pacific)

In [6]: datetime_in_pdt = datetime.datetime(2015,3,11,9,0,0, tzinfo=pacific)

In [7]: datetime_in_pdt.astimezone(utczone)
Out[7]: datetime.datetime(2015, 3, 11, 16, 53, tzinfo=<UTC>)

In [8]: datetime_in_pst.astimezone(utczone)
Out[8]: datetime.datetime(2015, 3, 4, 16, 53, tzinfo=<UTC>)

```
pytz documentation mentions this limitation http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#localized-times-and-date-arithmetic.


The arrow library works around this issue as it does not rely on pytz for this and other reasons. It does still have other [big issues with DST](https://github.com/crsmithdev/arrow/milestone/1) and I'm not sure if incorporating arrow here could solve this particular problem as there is a heavy dependency on dateutil in charge of generating the occurrences.

```python
In [1]: import arrow

In [2]: datetime_in_pst = arrow.get(2015, 3, 4, 9, 0, 0, 0, 'US/Pacific')

In [3]: datetime_in_pdt = arrow.get(2015, 3, 11, 9, 0, 0, 0, 'US/Pacific')

In [4]: datetime_in_pst.to('UTC')
Out[4]: <Arrow [2015-03-04T17:00:00+00:00]>

In [5]: datetime_in_pdt.to('UTC')
Out[5]: <Arrow [2015-03-11T16:00:00+00:00]>
```

Yes, that would work. There are a few ways I can think of this might be done, actually:

- A `StreamHandler` subclass could simply filter out escape codes if it's not writing to a TTY (a little hacky, but also the simplest option).

- It might be possible for a `Handler` to switch between multiple formatters based on the stream being a TTY, but I don't know if that can be made to work with the configuration loaders (would mean defining the format twice).

- The `ColoredFormatter` could implement some way to enable/disable colors when formatting a message, and then a `StreamHandler` subclass could pass that information to `.format()` as an extra optional parameter (most complex, but also not as flawed as the other options).

The problem was that I sent username and message as array. ws4redis sent this to redis, so I got such messages:
`127.0.0.1:6379> GET chat:broadcast:test`
`"alibek,q,qq"`
Well redis sees user ("alibek") and message ("q") are separated by a colon, but there is also third item ("qq"). So redis just doesn't do anything to it.
### Solution
Use `JSON.stringufy` at `sendMessage()` and `JSON.parse` at `receiveMessage()`.

class TimeLineChart(widgets.LineChart):
    # Display flow of CTRs during experiment
    title = 'Click-through Rate Time Series'
    leg_queryset = Experiment.objects.filter(group__name__isnull=False).values_list('name', 'group__name')
    val_queryset = Experiment.objects.filter(group__name__isnull=False).filter(goal__act_subject__isnull=False) \

    class Chartist:
        # visual tuning

            'axisX': {
                'labelOffset': {

                    'y': 10
                },

            },
            'lineSmooth':False,
            'chartPadding': {
                'top': 50,
                'left': 50,
                'right': 50,
                'bottom': 20,
            }
        }

    # to specify experiment period
    @classmethod
    def elapsed_time(cls, exp_name):
        started = [datetime['start_time'] for datetime in Experiment.objects.filter(name=exp_name).values('start_time')][0]
        today = timezone.now()
        elapsed_time = (today - started).days + 3
        return elapsed_time

    # to specify experiment name, group name
    def legend(self):
        return [exp_name + ' ' + group_name for exp_name, group_name in self.leg_queryset]
    '''
    legend queryset format
    <ExperimentQuerySet [('exp1', 'A'), ('exp1', 'B'), ('exp2', 'control'), ('exp2', 'test')]>
    '''

    # to represent values on x-axis such as date. ex) 2018-01-18
    def labels(self):
        today = timezone.now().date()
        labels = [(today - timezone.timedelta(days=x)).strftime('%Y-%m-%d') for x in range(self.elapsed_time)]
        return labels
    '''
    labels format
    ['2018-01-18',
     '2018-01-17',
     '2018-01-16',
     '2018-01-15',
     '2018-01-14',
     '2018-01-13',
     '2018-01-12']
    '''

    # to represent values on y-axis which is calculated by values()
    def series(self):
        series = []
        for legend_idx in range(len(self.legend)-1, -1, -1):
            alist = []
            for label in self.labels:
                result = self.values.get(label, {})[legend_idx]
                alist.append(result)
            series.append(alist)
        return series
    '''
    series format
    [[0.6, 0.6, 0.5, 0, 0, 0, 0],
     [0.9, 0.778, 0.857, 0, 0, 0, 0],
     [0.538, 0.571, 0.5, 0, 0, 0, 0],

    # to calculate values such as CTR. ex) 0.725

            for exp_name, group_name, act_subject in self.val_queryset:
                alist.append(kpi.CTR(exp_name, group_name, act_subject, label))
            values[label] = alist
        return values
    '''
    values format
    {'2018-01-12': [0, 0, 0, 0],
     '2018-01-13': [0, 0, 0, 0],
     '2018-01-14': [0, 0, 0, 0],

        }

1. If you want to use this markdown editor for `/admin/` _(Django Administration)_ and outside it. you can only use it on `models`

```python
from django.db import models
from martor.models import MartorField

class Question(models.Model, HitCountMixin):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=False)
    description = MartorField()
```

2. If you want to show this markdown editor only for your `/admin/` _(Django Administration)_, you can use this in your file `admin.py`:

```python
from django.db import models
from django.contrib import admin

from martor.widgets import AdminMartorWidget
from yourapp.models import Question

class QuestionAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    formfield_overrides = {
        models.TextField: {'widget': AdminMartorWidget},
    }

admin.site.register(Question, QuestionAdmin)
```

3. But, if you only want to show this markdown editor outside `/admin/` _(Django Administration)_. you can use this:

```python
class QuestionForm(forms.ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        model = Question
        fields = ['title', 'description']

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.user = kwargs.pop('user')
        super(QuestionForm, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.fields['description'] = MartorFormField()
```

I'm running into this issue too. Hopefully this provides more clarity:

When hooking up the flaky nose plugin and running a single failing test that inherits from Django's TestCase, here's the order of events:
```
>>> _pre_setup
>>> test run #1
>>> flaky startTest
>>> flaky stopTest
>>> _pre_setup
>>> test run #2
>>> flaky startTest
>>> flaky stopTest
>>> _pre_setup
>>> test run #3
>>> flaky startTest
>>> flaky stopTest
>>> _post_teardown
>>> _post_teardown
>>> _post_teardown
```

The important piece here is that `_post_teardown` isn't called in between test retries. This causes a buffet of errors in our test suite. I can provide stacktraces if you'd like, but I'm not sure they'd be helpful.

One way to fix this could be to change how tests are re-run. We could get a handle on the suite that's running inside [`prepareTest`](http://nose.readthedocs.io/en/latest/plugins/interface.html#nose.plugins.base.IPluginInterface.prepareTest) and then when tests fail/error, conditionally add the test to the suite using [`addTest`](https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/blob/7c26ad1e6b7d308cafa328ad34736d34028c122a/nose/suite.py#L67-L68). I tested this out locally to make sure that the tests actually get re-run when added to the suite via `addTest`, and it's working so far. Would y'all consider this approach? If so, I'd happily try making the change.

1. For any task B, which depends on task A (marked as only_run_latest), the dependencies for an instance of B should be met if any instance of A has succeeded on or after the execution date.
2. Avoid scheduling multiple instances of a task that has been marked as only_run_latest and prioritize the most recent execution date.

@qmaruf yep, the high level idea is that we'd want to rotate the log file.  This is typically implemented in one of two ways:

1. External software that manages log files can rename the current log file to a new name, then send some kind of signal to the service to tell it to re-open its log file
2. Modify the service itself so that it has some kind of rotation policy.

We can support both of these things.  The high level plan is:

1. Add a function that can reopen the log file.  This is logically as simple as closing the old log file handle and then opening the configured log file again.  The existing code for opening the log file is towards the top of main.c; it should be moved into log.c.  Care should be taken to ensure that we don't lose log events while making this switch.
2. Add a watchman command that will trigger the log file to be re-opened.  This will facilitate controlling log file rotation via software like `logrotate`; it would just run `watchman log-reopen`
3. Add a configuration option that specifies: 
 1. how often to check the log file size in seconds
 2. the maximum size
4. If size checking is enabled (the checking interval is non-zero), every interval seconds, compare the size.  If the size is larger than desired, rename the log file by appending the current unix timestamp to the log file name and then reopen the log file.

I consider the following things to be non-goals for watchman; our focus is on answering questions about the filesystem rather than re-implementing features that exist in other widely deployed software:

1. Configuring an alternative or more flexible way of naming the moved log files
2. Limiting the number of re-opened log files (I'm on the fence about this, but am leaning towards saying that the user can script cleaning this up for themselves, outside of watchman.  Other software exists with the sole purpose of performing this kind of cleanup)

It might be a bug in `python-jose`. See [the doc string for `jws.verify`](https://github.com/mpdavis/python-jose/blob/7403dffbf9d8980c43c9ea40e4c580035d7b2fce/jose/jws.py#L60)
It says the payload is supposed to be a `str`. 

However, the `payload` is ultimately coming from [here](https://github.com/mpdavis/python-jose/blob/7403dffbf9d8980c43c9ea40e4c580035d7b2fce/jose/jws.py#L196) which is, ultimately, the output of calling `base64.urlsafe_b64decode` which in Python 3 *always* returns a byte string. 

```python
>>> base64.urlsafe_b64decode('cGV0ZXI=')
b'peter'
>>> base64.urlsafe_b64decode(b'cGV0ZXI=')
b'peter'
```

So I think we need to account for this when we send the payload to `json.loads`.

Networks should not be able to be part of multiple VRFs - there could be overlaps, different mask lengths, same mask lengths and it would probably screw with auditing and reporting down the track. 

Think about it this way - Let's say 192.168.0.0/24 exists in three seperate VPNs/networks/VRFs. I should have _three_ seperate network objects in the database because they really are _three_ seperate networks. On a router I can't assign more than one VPNV4 network per interface. I.E. I need a sub-interface per VPN at the MPLS to IP boundary.

So I have a device, it has multiple logical sub-interfaces, each sub-interface has a VPNV4 network attached.

One obvious use case is that I want an operator to be able to search for a straight IP subnet and get that network in all RDs. I want to also be able to search for a specific VPNV4 network, or all networks within an RD.

I'm not enough of programer to even know where to look in the code to see what your database structure looks like. But looking at your documentation again, I like @coxley 's idea for the hard coded RD field for every network. The default value for RD field for existing and newly created networks would be 'none', noting that 0:0 is a valid RD.

I'm going with 'RD' being the key because the RD is transitive - the VRF name is locally significant to the device only (although of course you usually make your VRF name the same on every device).

- Support for latest Firefox releases is pretty urgent.
- We should also support older Firefox versions at least for now.
- The less configuration users need to do when opening the browser the better.
- The current configuration system is not very user friendly:
  - There are separate `Create WebDriver` and `Open Browser` keywords.
 - Desired capabilities need to be passed in as a dictionary.

Related to the last two points, I would be really happy if we could redesign opening/configuring browsers so that we'd have only one keyword and all configuration could be passed in directly using `name=value` syntax. This would obviously cause backwards compatibility problems, so the new system needed to be considerably better than the current one for changes to be worth the effort. Most likely we needed to first introduce a new keyword, then deprecate current `Create WebDriver` and `Open Browser`, and finally perhaps rename the new keyword to `Open Browser`. `.env` is used for code-specific environment variables, not system ones. Pipenv is configured by your system, not your code.

@uranusjr it sounds like what you're saying is that Pipfile only needs to explicitly list project dependencies if they are not already being captured by a packaging tool like Setuptools or Flit (via setup.py or pyproject.toml)

For example, if setup.py looks like:
```
install_requires=['A'],
extras_require={
    'dev': ['B'],
},
```
Then the Pipfile only needs the following:
```
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"


[packages]

"e1839a8" = {path = ".", editable = true}


[dev-packages]

"e1839a8" = {path = ".", extras = ["dev"], editable = true}
```
Running `pipenv install` would install dependency A for production, and `pipenv install --dev` would install dependencies A & B for development.

If someone is already using Setuptools or Flit, is there ever any reason why dependencies should be added into the Pipfile under `[packages]` or `[dev-packages]`?  Looking at [Requests](https://github.com/requests/requests) as an example, it's not obvious to me why the development dependencies are listed explicitly in Pipfile under `[dev-packages]`, but the `test_requirements` dependencies are all captured in setup.py.

It seems like the only reason why you would **need** to add dependencies explicitly to Pipfile is if you're **not** using Setuptools or Flit.  Is this correct?  Are there reasons why this is not true?

```

Of course ar should be gcc-ar in this case, otherwise the build fails. This indicates that `CMAKE_AR` and `CMAKE_RANLIB` set in the root CMakeLists.txt aren't propagated correctly to the externals (maybe this is a bug, see https://cmake.org/Bug/view.php?id=15547).

As a consequence, we have to keep the variables in CMakeLists.txt as well as both `COMMON_STATICLIB_CMAKE_DEFINITIONS_OPTIMIZED` and
`COMMON_STATICLIB_CONFIGURE_DEFINITIONS_OPTIMIZED_STR` in `CommonCompilerConfig.cmake`.

Turning off optimizations makes it go away, but turning on optimizations and still linking against MSVC*.dll results in a working binary as well. The fault is occurring where the compiler tries to inline memcpy.

My guess was right. Even without knowing the format, it is easy to improve compression ratio.

1. Split odd and even bytes, e.g. with python script
```
f = open("input", "rb")
K = 2
d = [[] for k in range(K)]
i = 0
b = f.read(1)
while b != "":
  d[i % K].append(b)
  i = i + 1
  b = f.read(1)
f.close()

f = open("output", "wb")
for k in range(K):
  f.write(''.join(d[k]))
f.close()
```

2. Compress
3. Profit!
 - Zopfli: 1,847,682 bytes (~22% smaller)
 - Brotli: 1,172,693 bytes (~29% smaller)

I believe that with smarter transform results will be even better.

Since you have `localhost` in there, all commands from patronictl are send to the local node...

So, Pythonically I don't need much benefit over:

```python
while True:
   messages = subscription.pull(return_immediately=False)
   # Do stuff with messages
```

vs

```python
def do_stuff_with_messages(messages):
    # ....

subscription.pull(callback=do_stuff_with_messages)
```

Callbacks in Python are usually reserved for asynchronous programming. It seems in your FR that you expected calling `subscription.pull(callback=DoWork)` to not block. Unfortunately, that gets into the realm of async and there there are a multitude of ways to do that in Python and it would seem unfair for us to choose one way and limit our users.

For example, if someone wanted to use gevent to do what you said, they could easily implement that via:

```python
def worker_thread(subscription, callback):
    while True:
        messages = subscription.pull()
        for message in messages:
            callback(message)


thread = gevent.spawn(worker_thread, subscription, some_callback)
```

`ProtobufEntry.payload_pb` is an instance of `google.protobuf.any_pb2.Any`.  Per the [protobuf docs](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/reference/python-generated#any), you can unpack it into a known message type via:

```python
from my_package import my_message_pb2

if entry.payload_pb.Is(my_messsage_pb2.MyMessage.DESCRIPTOR):
    message = my_message_pb2.MyMessage()
    entry.payload_pb.Unpack(message)
    # do something with message
```

I got a workaround for this.. Apparently when using SAM (aws cloudformation deploy) it uses the transform (Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31) rule set in your template to generate all kind of "extra" resources. One of them is a `aws::ApiGateway::stage` resource. The name of this resource is `ServerlessApiStage` where ServerlessApi is the name of the `AWS::Serverless::Api` resource.

I've ben messing with this problem too. The reason it doesn't work the way we want is probably easiest explained by trying to do the same thing in the AWS Console.

If you view your API's Resources, and dig down into one of the "Integration Request"s that you're working with, you can see the options that are built from this uri.

The lambda ARN literally lets you put in the text "${stageVariables.XYZ}". This is a special feature of cloudformation, letting you route to different lambda functions based on the stage.

The rest of the fields, however, do not allow such dynamic inputs. This also leads us to consider the difference between the API Resource definition and the Stage definition. SAM abstracts a lot of that out for us with the "AWS::Serverless::Api" type, but under the hood, those two things are separate. The swagger template is NOT rendered during stage generation, but during resource generation.

https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/8#issuecomment-290212883 is probably the closest thing I have found to being able to use those sort of variables dynamically in the swagger file.

Thanks again for your patience, the latest update here is that we're leaning towards is creating a new `CONNECTION_ERROR` value of `error_type` (along with some new `error_code` values). The benefit is that this keeps the errors clearly distinguishable from the `PLAID_ERROR` value of `error_type`, but lets us morph known request error cases into the same `PlaidError` python class for easier code handling.

The next step is to pin down the error codes (to keep it consistent about it across client libraries for different languages) and figure out whether we want to roll this into a major version bump vs. a minor bump (concern here would be around breaking any existing error handling which assumes the `request` library errors, tbd whether I'm just being overly cautious with respect to that though).

Hello Matthias,

Thanks for the report! I've not been able to reproduce this issue in the [add_ad_group_bid_modifier.py](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/examples/adwords/v201806/advanced_operations/add_ad_group_bid_modifier.py) example. I suspect the underlying cause is that you're using the **CampaignBidModifierService** in your code snippet.

The AdGroupBidModifierService has a [Platform](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/reference/v201806/AdGroupBidModifierService.Platform) criterion, whereas CampaignBidModifierService [does not](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/reference/v201806/CampaignBidModifierService.Criterion).

Regards,
Mark

@iansmith Sorry you got tripped up on this - we discussed this in today's issue review and think that this is a pretty infrequent edge case, and logic required to fix this would be fragile and hard to test.

So I have been looking into this, and there are a few things that may be happening:

1) If the service gets into the state `failed` during a `deisctl start|stop|restart` operation, the deisctl client may hang even if the service continues to function. #4026 fixes this (I just finished it, and we'll get it merged as soon as its been tested)

2) Several people are noticing a case where builder takes a CRAZY amount of time to stand up -- hours, in one case. This has something to do with docker hub, and we're looking at it.

> If additional registries are nothing but warm standbys, what advantage (if any) does creating those offer over, say, simply preseeding all nodes with the Deis component images?

The failover is slightly faster because you don't have to wait for the registry to boot, but otherwise the advantage is minimal.

As for `deis-router`, that is up to the load balancer. The load balancer will route requests and pass it off to one of the routers for processing, so the routers are a true master/master setup. The registry *can* be a master/master setup, but components like the builder require a single endpoint. Since we don't have internal DNS discovery, we use the "masterLock" logic to force `/deis/registry/host` to be one of the hosts until it goes down, which then another registry will take its place. See https://github.com/deis/deis/pull/2434 for the justification behind the masterLock publishing logic.

> I tried getting the status of the ceph cluster with ceph -s, but it never returns the status, just shows ping-like ouput, then eventually times-out.

This is what bothers me - clusters should almost always be recoverable, but if you can't communicate with the cluster, there's nothing you can do. 

I've actually been thinking about this last issue. @mgartner, could you enter a **daemon** container and gist `cat /etc/ceph/ceph.conf`? I bet you won't see any of your new monitors there. If there are no monitors in that file that are active and running in your cluster, no `ceph` commands will work. 

You should be able to get these files to be re-templated by `deisctl restart store-daemon`, but we should investigate a way to re-template this these with the daemon running. This isn't possible with the way the /bin/boot scripts are currently authored (as we need to exec to replace the process with the monitor or daemon process), but perhaps #2701 gives us that opportunity.

You thus form a feature vector of length K, where K is the number of shapelets in your dictionary. Another option is to take the argmin of this vector to reduce the number of features. 

Moreover, you can use these shapelets to transform your data: [paper](https://ueaeprints.uea.ac.uk/40201/1/LinesKDD2012.pdf)

We don't support having mixed `stepUnits` for the same variable and you can easily work-around that using the `filter_by_keys` keyword argument in `open_dataset`, as documented in the README. In this sense the issue is a duplicate of #2, please move there if you have additional comments. I'll close this one.

For example:
```
>>> xarray_store.open_dataset(
...    'cosmo-d2_germany_regular-lat-lon_single-level_2018073000_001_ASWDIFD_S.grib2',
...    filter_by_keys={'stepUnits': 1})

(this assumes you have a working config file setup already)
```

import pyeapi

# create a node object by specifying the node to work with
node = pyeapi.connect_to('veos01')

# use the config method to  enable tap agg mode (note this will likely be a disruptive change):
node.config(['tap aggregation', 'mode exclusive'])
[{}, {}]

# enable tap port
node.config(['interface Ethernet1', 'switchport tap default group veos1'])
[{}, {}]

node.config(['interface Ethernet2', 'switchport tool group veos1'])
[{}, {}]

```

So I've done some investigation and the problem isn't that they need to be closed (as that causes problems with the object being attached to a closed session) but that the session needs to rollback or commit when the session is returned.

I've not tested within this library but the following may work for a session:

https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroachdb-python/blob/45537bfae46cdaad8d10c77cdbc86563c0fb1f0a/cockroachdb/sqlalchemy/transaction.py#L29-L31

```
    elif isinstance(transactor, sqlalchemy.orm.sessionmaker):
        session = transactor(autocommit=True)
        try:
          result = _txn_retry_loop(session, callback)
          session.commit()
          return result
        except ...:
          session.rollback()
          raise ...
```

Our non-cockroach approach is to create a context manager:

```
@contextmanager
@sqlalchemy_exception_handler
def wrap_run_transaction(db):
    """Provide a transactional scope around a series of operations."""
    session = db.create_scoped_session()
    try:
        yield session
        session.commit()
    except Exception as err:
        session.rollback()
        raise err
    # do not close, just make sure transactions are complete
```

and use as:

```
with wrap_run_transaction(db) as session:
            _set_file_state(session, file_uuid, 'Uploaded', size_bytes=file_size)
```

Inspiration taken from here: http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_basics.html#when-do-i-construct-a-session-when-do-i-commit-it-and-when-do-i-close-it

Not closing is intentional as otherwise lazy loading will fail on objects if used generically for read and write.

I'm currently rewriting the PR, and I'd like some input on what to do with `context`.

Based on prior discussion, the `context` argument in `import_loop` means "trusted data" and should be renamed to reflect that. That much is clear.

The other usage ("arbitrary user data") could otherwise continue to be called "context", but I think we'd like all conversion functions to accept such arbitrary data, including `import_loop`. Well, we can't just suddenly change the meaning of `context` in the `import_loop` signature from *trusted data* to *arbitrary user data*.

That said, once `env` is available, the ability to pass options as individual arguments is just a convenience:

```python
instance.import_data({'name': 'bintoro'}, partial=True, strict=True)
```

```python
instance.import_data({'name': 'bintoro'}, env=ConfigObject(partial=True, strict=True))
```

The above statments are equivalent. In the former, `import_loop` just takes care of constructing the `env` object.

If we forgo this convenience for `context`, then I think it's OK to continue to use that name, since it won't appear in the `import_loop` (or any other) signature any longer.

The other option is to eliminate "context" throughout the library and replace it with something like "private", "app", "user", ...

(Basically, `env` already *is* a context container where applications could put anything. But I think it's a good idea to have a dedicated object to keep the user namespace separate from reserved parameters.)

My current thinking is that we could just drop `context` from function signatures because
  * it's not the most commonly-used feature
  * when you need it, creating the `env` object is very easy
  * "context" is a pretty good name for user data.

> Why? We could support a query that allows the client to find out whether or not a given domain has any missing zonefiles earlier than some transaction. This query is cheap for the indexer, and cheap for the client.

Gotcha.  I misunderstood what you were asking--I thought you were suggesting that the client fetch all zone files and replay the subdomain history locally.  This makes sense though---it would let a client detect dishonesty on the domain operator's part.

> In general, I think it's possible that blockstackd refusing to process subdomain updates if a zonefile is missing can work, but it's really important that if that's the case, we have a plan for dealing with missing zonefiles due to errors or bugs (after all, there are many missing zonefiles currently) before shipping that behavior.

I think the design decision we're trying to make is whether or not we want the zonefile arrival schedule to influence the subdomain index's view of a subdomain's address.

I think @kantai is saying that the view does not need to be stable---a subdomain's address can retroactively change with the arrival of a missing zonefile.  The arguments for this are that (1) it allows subdomain processing to continue in the face of missing zone files, and (2) if we require the domain to process all address transfers, then the client can efficiently detect whether or not the subdomain view is stable based on whether or not the domain is missing any zone files after the subdomain was created.

My argument was that the view does need to be stable---a subdomain's address cannot retroactively change due to a zone file arrival.  My argument was that doing otherwise allows an attacker to trick other users into trusting the wrong key for a subdomain by controlling the arrival schedule.  The downside of this approach is that any missing zone files from the domain(s) that process address changes for a subdomain cannot get lost; otherwise subdomains from that domain would not be able to be processed.

Having talked this through more, I'm more inclined to agree with @kantai for the following reasons:
* Only the subdomain owner can create address-change TXT records in the first place, so a user signing up for a subdomain is unlikely to reorg her own subdomain history (doing so would break her applications).
* A user would not create a subdomain on a domain name that is missing zone files in the first place (otherwise their subdomain would not resolve).
* A user should not be blocked from updating their zone files based on the creator domain's bad behavior.

Nevertheless, it should still be the case that only the domain name can process address changes.  The reason is that clients still need an efficient way to detect subdomain view instability.  Constraining the set of domains that can process address changes to just the domain that created it would mean that a client only needs to check the domain name's zone file history to verify that all zone files are present.  If we did not do this, then *any* missing zone file could be a source of view instability.

> 1. It's file/module level code that runs during the loading of the module. I think that this is a poor place to put code that is actually the functionality of the module, rather than the setting up of the module.

I agree, my example is mostly to illustrate the concept. The actual implementation should address this issue.

> 2. Handlers are the place to switch on/off states (flags). This is already a pattern that has been established, and this change would further complicate the set of concepts that a charm author would need to know about.

I'd argue that `@preflight` actually complicates the concepts. To use `@preflight`, you need to know 
1. how flags work,
2. how the reactive framework works internally,
3. how hooks work.

Ideally, users should only have to know about 1. so the more we solve with the "flags" concept, the easier the reactive framework is to use. This complexity has the potential to leak to higher layers, it isn't limited to the developer of the layer that actually uses `@preflight`.

Furthermore, what we try to create with `@preflight` are flags that magically appear or disappear outside of the "normal" functionality of the reactive framework. With `if_this_then_remove`, we make these changes explicit.

Lastly, the `if_this_then_remove` proposal doesn't only solve the `@preflight` use-case. It also enables developers to create layers that validate config. See issue https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/issues/98

Thanks for taking the time to reply. One pain point having a `setup_requires` introduces is installing a package that has a dependency with a `setup_requires` from a local index no longer works. Something like:
```
pip install --no-index --find-links file://./packages package-x.y.z.tar.gz
```
Where `./packages` has all required packages (even those defined in `setup_requires`). You have to explicitly install any and all `setup_requires` dependencies before attempting to install the desired package, otherwise `setuptools` will attempt to download the package ignoring the local index.

@mjschultz I think I went into a similar issue as you do.

I think the main inconsistency with the `tzlocal` class is that some of its methods reference `time` module in real time (e.g., `.tzname()`) while some use the cached values upon module import (e.g., `.utcoffset()`).  Therefore, caching `tzname` just like we do for `_std_offset` or `_dst_offset` feels like the right way of solving the problem.

On the other hand, in my opinion, fix the timezone information for each `tzlocal` instances might be a good idea too. So I prepared a patch #107 to achieve this. Then I realized that it won't fix your problem where `time.tzname` is still not fixed during instance initialization.

Would please take a look and see if caching `_tzname` in the `__init__` method makes sense to you. Also I'm not very familiar with the testing framework, would please check if your testing case can cover the changes in #107 as well?

Thanks!

These are all good questions. My responses below.

1. You do not _need_ to make the contiguous and starting at 0; the model will work find. However, it will allocate parameters for indices in-between that you don't use, which will make your model larger (and potentially more noisy).
2. Yes, you can, subject to the same caveat as above.
3. User ids here are _indices_ into the feature matrices. So if your user id is 100, your feature matrix will need to have at least 101 rows; but if you make the user id 0, you can have a feature matrix with a single row.
4. With the values you provided, `n_users = max([1, 1, 20, 20, 100]) + 1 = 101`.

Users and items are symmetrical in this model, so this applies equally. You may find my [recent changes](https://github.com/lyst/lightfm/commit/82b215456720cb27c11ee595767c680583bda043) to the documentation helpful here.

> But is there a use case for dropping out say the second and the third dimension?

I can think of use cases for many different `shared_axes` values:
* shared_axes=(): standard dropout
* shared_axes=(0): batch-wise dropout (use the same dropout mask across axis 0)
* shared_axes=(2,3): drop full feature maps (use the same dropout mask across axes 2 and 3)
* shared_axes=(1,2): drop full frequency bands of spectrograms (use the same dropout mask across axes 1 and 2)

We wouldn't want a separate dropout layer class for each variant. We could have a `spatial_dropout()` shortcut, though.

I see a middle path forward.  Given the following constraints:

1) The core developers prefer to develop in python3, because they have the vision for the long-term stability, maintenance, and adoption of the code.
2) The strong majority of the end-users will require fully featured support for python2 in the short term.  The Allen Institute requires this support as soon as possible, and cannot use this software in production without this support (@dyf correct me if I am wrong here).
3) We need to assist the community’s transition to python3, for longevity.

To satisfy all of these I propose:

1) The core code (dev branch, and all PR’s to it) be developed in python3, but not including any features that are not somehow back-portable to python2. CI will run for dev in python3 only, however all features must have a back-ported python2 implementation that still pass tests (even if the backport gets kinda messy, like in https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/pull/60 src/form/build/map.py).  
2) We maintain a second dev branch (I vote to call it dev6) that supports python2.7 and python3 under CI (but not earlier 2.x). This branch is downstream of dev and also supported by the core dev team.  After alpha ends in November, dev6 is the basis of master, because many end-users in the short term will need this functionality (Constraint 2 above), however it should also support python3 so that we can facilitate community transition to python3 (Constraint 3 above), while still being fully featured.  

This addresses the two main issues of maintaining two separate forks: guaranteeing equivalent functionality (for this to work, dev must maintain high test coverage, maybe >90% seems right to me; @ajtritt and @oruebel have established a great precedent for this already), and the distribution difficulties associated with having python2 users aimed at one branch and python3 users aimed at another (both are aimed at a python2/3 master).  This will cost us the ability to implement any features in the short run that cannot be back-ported, but these would cost us our (mostly python2) user-base anyways.

At a pre-defined later date (I propose some time in 2020, when python2 is officially phased out), we drop the guarantee of full-featured python2 support.  We make a major revision number increment, master becomes downstream of dev, and we get back a nice and neat python3 codebase to support long-term development (again, Constraint 1 above), without sacrificing any functionality.  dev6 gets renamed to legacy, and is maintained ad-hoc with a warning that it is not guaranteed to be fully featured (although at the time of transition, it will be, by definition).  Also this point, obviously, the backwards-compatible feature restriction is removed on code PR’ed to dev.  If we all agree to this, we should additionally make this decision prominent on a milestone page on the repo, along with the rationale behind it as early as possible (and definitely before November).

Then we make a plan to deal with python4 :)

```python
import trio

async def silly_server(stream):
    data = bytearray()
    while True:
        data += await stream.receive_some(4096)
        if b"\r\n\r\n" in data:
            break
    await stream.send_all(
        b"HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nTest: \x00\r\nContent-Length: 2\r\n\r\nXX"
    )

async def main():
    await trio.serve_tcp(silly_server, 8888)

trio.run(main)

One thing I've noticed is that -- particularly on Windows -- monitoring memory usage in a Python script via task manager or top only tells you if a leak is possible, but not if it actually exists.  Python can hold onto memory longer than it's reachable from the Python runtime (that is, the garbage collector does not need to collect as soon as the refcount hits 0).

It's always a good idea to toss a `gc.collect()` somewhere to see if the memory profile stops increasing continuously.  But even there it's not a sure bet.

If it *is* leaking, [1] then it must be somewhere in the C/C++ code.  In that case, `valgrind` can help track it down.

[1] Pure Python doesn't technically *leak* unless the GC is broken, and it almost certainly isn't.  A Python "leak" is always some sort of collection that is accumulating objects (lists, sets, and dicts are the most common suspects) regularly without purging them.  But those objects are *always* reachable, so they can be `del`ed... if you know where to find them :).

> What we want for ipmag.aniso_magic is to take in some arguments, quietly create few plots, and then return them. However, pmagplotlib is very much written to produce plots interactively with matplotlib. The code is full of calls to plt.show, etc., and pmagplotlib never actually returns Figure objects, it just shows or saves them. This is a huge mess for the notebooks (or the GUIs, for that matter). matplotlib has an object-oriented API that for creating plots without all the interactive display logic, but pmagplotlib would need to be re-written.

Thank you so much for the information. I had missed the nuance in the documentation that STL containers are only assignable in their entirety unless you explicitly bind them as opaque objects.

For 2 and 3, this seems like an unfortunate limitation. Previously, my code featured SIP bindings, which allowed you to specify arbitrary "get" and "set" code for bound objects, so you could write accessors for primitive pointers/arrays that turned them into standard list objects without pulling in numpy. I may explore writing a simple wrapper class for primitives with a bound __setitem__ and __getitem__ method that hopefully will be a little more intuitive (at least, for me).

For 4, I was referring to the lack of documentation on binding c-style/non-STL arrays or pointers-as-arrays. Following the docs explanations for standard class and function bindings, it seemed like a logical step to me to be able to bind "int array[100]" by writing .def("array", &MyClass::array). 

For dynamically allocated memory, I can intuitively tell there's no good way to get a size to create a list, but it still doesn't leave me with a clear picture of what I have to do to bind it. From what I could find, others simply wrote full set/get methods for the arrays in the c++ code and bound those instead, so I think I'm not the only one facing this question. It would be great to have my hand held and told "this is exactly what you do to bind int[100] to a Python list". You suggest numpy, which is perhaps the "correct" solution, but for someone newly coming into pybind11, I assumed numpy was for heavier duty number manipulation and didn't explore it as a solution for my problem.

This is a duplicate of #5255, so closing the issue in favor of keeping the discussion in one place. In short, Certbot has a bug in parsing of dysfunctional rewrite rules (`RewriteCond` without a following `RewriteRule`). A workaround for the time being is to comment out or disable the existing `RewriteCond` directives, as they do not do anything presently.

# domain2-le-ssl.conf

<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
<virtualhost *:443>
ServerName domain2.org
ServerAlias www.domain2.org
ServerAdmin me@someemailadress.com

DocumentRoot /home/domain2admin/domain2.org/html/

</home/domain2admin/domain2.org/html/>
  Options Indexes FollowSymLinks Includes
  AllowOverride none
  Require all granted
</directory>

ProxyPass    '/xxx/'    'http://www.domain2.org:8180/xxx/'
ProxyPassReverse '/xxx/'    'http://www.domain2.org:8180/xxx/'

CustomLog ${APACHE_LOG_DIR}/access.log combined
ErrorLog ${APACHE_LOG_DIR}/error.log
SSLCertificateFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain1.com/cert.pem
SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain1.com/privkey.pem
Include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-apache.conf
SSLCertificateChainFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain1.com/chain.pem
</virtualhost>
</IfModule>

From your `curl`, looks like you tried to do that from webUI, right? This is caused by the fact that Raiden only accepts a `total_deposit` (idempotent) value for the deposited amount in a given channel. To be able to set a desired amount, the client (in the case, webui) should fetch the current channel balance from Raiden API, and use it to calculate the new total_deposit. As I can see from the logs, looks like your previous balance was 10, and you tried setting total_deposit=5, which would cause an amount to be deposited of -5, which is invalid. The webUI fetches the current balance straight from the raiden API, so it may be caused by either a outdated value (there's still some issues with events not being correctly picked, or being doubly picked, like #1910, which causes some issues with internal state), or some pending previous operation, which wasn't picked yet by the node. We may improve that with some locks soon.

I've converted Karen Simonyan's VGG-16 and VGG-19 models here:

https://github.com/jcjohnson/pytorch-vgg

The converted models are available here:

VGG-16: https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/jcjohns-models/vgg16-00b39a1b.pth

VGG-19: https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/jcjohns-models/vgg19-d01eb7cb.pth

These are exact conversions of Karen's Caffe checkpoints, so they expect BGR images in the range [0, 255] with the BGR value [103.939, 116.779, 123.68] subtracted from each pixel; I suspect that this is different from the pretrained AlexNet and ResNets in the model zoo.

Feel free to add them to the model zoo if you want, but it would be convenient if all pretrained models used the same preprocessing.

As part of this change we might need to change behaviour of the `read_documents` function

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/5f7211d2eb26ac10d6dfb754418d4bd3079cdf7a/datacube/utils/__init__.py#L241-L252

Currently it produces a generator of `(path, parsed_yaml_doc)` from a bunch of filesystem paths. I think it should return url instead of just plain path, and this url should include fragment part in the case of stacked netcdf file, and arguably for multi-part yaml documents also. Switching to url will also allow future extension for non-local sources like `http://` or `s3://` . 

Most users of this function ignore path output anyway, or use it for logging purposes only. As far as I can tell only `dataset update/add` use returned path for anything but logging, and the first they do is to convert it to a uri.

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/a69dfff5154042eb9192629978a306f77ce9be49/datacube/scripts/dataset.py#L132-L133

I created a completely fresh Centos 6.1 install in a virtual machine. Here are the commands I gave. 

```
sudo yum groupinstall 'Development Tools'
sudo yum install python-devel
sudo yum install libuuid-devel

wget http://download.zeromq.org/zeromq-2.1.11.tar.gz
gunzip zeromq-2.1.11.tar.gz
tar -xf zeromq-2.1.11.tar
cd zeromq-2.1.11
./configure
make
sudo make install
sudo ldconfig

wget https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/downloads/pyzmq-2.1.11.tar.gz
gunzip pyzmq-2.1.11.tar.gz
tar -xf pyzmq-2.1.11.tar
cd pyzmq-2.1.11
python setup.py configure --zmq=/usr/local
python setup.py build
sudo python setup.py install

```




The --zmq=/usr/local is required in order to get the

    python setup.py configure --zmq=/usr/local

command to work.
